citation.title: Polyelectrolyte complex composite nanofiltration membranes fabricated in two steps with hydrocarbon-based sulfonated ionomer and polyethyleneimines

content: A B S T R A C T This study presents a novel approach to fabricating chlorine-stable nanofiltration (NF) membranes using a scalable two-step dip-coating process using the hydrocarbon-based ionomer Nexar and branched poly­ethylenimine (PEI) as polyelectrolyte. A thin-film composite (TFC) membrane was first formed by casting Nexar onto an aminated polyacrylonitrile (aPAN) support, followed by complexation with PEI to create a poly­electrolyte complex (PEC) film, to yield PEC-NF membranes. The influence of solvent systems, drying conditions and cast film thickness on the performance of the Nexar TFC membrane has been studied initially. With the best performing Nexar TFC prototype, it has been found that molecular weight (MW) of PEI has a profound influence on PEC-NF membrane structure and performance. A comprehensive characterization of the membrane structures has been performed by scanning electron microscopy, atomic force microscopy, TOF-SIMS analyses, as well as zeta potential and contact angle measurements. Compared to the TFC precursor, the PEC-NF membranes exhibited improved multivalent salt rejection as well as higher rejection of sucrose and organic micropollutants, particularly for neutral and hydrophobic compounds, at either reduced (low MW PEI) or slightly increased (high MW PEI) permeance. All membrane variants demonstrated excellent stability under prolonged exposure to active chlorine (500 ppm NaOCl, pH 9).

content: sed (high MW PEI) permeance. All membrane variants demonstrated excellent stability under prolonged exposure to active chlorine (500 ppm NaOCl, pH 9). Increasing feed salt concentration yielded markedly decreasing salt rejection and increasing solution permeance. Remarkably, the rejection of neutral solutes such as sucrose (~90 % for PECNF with low MW PEI) was not influenced by feed salt concentration in the range of 0.2–10 g/L. This work highlights the potential of ionomer-based PEC membranes as a robust and tunable platform for advanced NF applications.

citation.title: Polyelectrolyte complex composite nanofiltration membranes fabricated in two steps with hydrocarbon-based sulfonated ionomer and polyethyleneimines

content: 1. Introduction Water scarcity is poised to become one of the major challenges of the coming decades, even in regions traditionally considered water-rich. Advanced water treatment technologies are required to address not only salinity removal but also the separation of organic micropollutants (OMP), such as pesticides, pharmaceuticals, hormones, and other endocrine-disrupting compounds [1,2]. Nanofiltration (NF) has gained attention in water treatment due to its moderate solute rejection com­bined with higher permeability and lower transmembrane pressure re­quirements compared to reverse osmosis. While NF can effectively remove many highly charged and sufficiently large OMPs, its rejection efficiency is highly solute-specific, and for some OMPs, incomplete rejection may lead to their accumulation in the treated effluent [3,4]. Most current NF membranes are thin-film composites (TFC), typically with some kind of polyamide (PA) layer formed through interfacial polymerization on a porous support membrane [5]. These membranes primarily separate solutes based on size exclusion, with molecular weight cut-offs (MWCO) typically in the range of 200–1000 Da. How­ever, charge-based mechanisms such as Donnan exclusion as well as dielectric exclusion can play a secondary role in enhancing the rejection, depending on membrane surface charge and solute charge [6]. Despite their effectiveness, PA TFC membranes face critical drawbacks related to fouling and chemical vulnerability.

A promising alternative to traditional polyamide NF membranes is the use of polyelectrolyte complexes (PEC) as the selective film [8]. PEC membranes can be fabricated using the layer-by-layer (LbL) assembly of oppositely charged polyelectrolytes on a porous support, offering tunable ion selectivity by varying polymer composition, the number of layers as well as deposition conditions, and resulting surface charge density [9]. Such multilayer polyelectrolyte membranes can be prepared to have a thin (≤100 nm) barrier film and have demonstrated good separation performance and stability in various applications. However, LbL assembly is a labor-intensive, multistep process, and the resulting structures, held together by reversible ionic bonds, may destabilize under high salt concentrations or extreme pH conditions. Indeed, recent studies have shown that highly saline environments can screen the electrostatic interactions in PEC films, leading to diminished separation performance or delamination from the support membrane [10].

citation.title: Polyelectrolyte complex composite nanofiltration membranes fabricated in two steps with hydrocarbon-based sulfonated ionomer and polyethyleneimines

content: s can screen the electrostatic interactions in PEC films, leading to diminished separation performance or delamination from the support membrane [10]. More­over, the mechanisms governing salt rejection and selectivity in PEC membranes are not yet fully understood, complicating predictive design and optimization [11]. To overcome the limitations of both PA TFC and conventional PEC multilayer membranes, recent research introduced a conceptually different approach for preparing PEC NF membranes involving a simple, two-step coating process using oppositely charged polymers [12]. In this method, an amphiphilic negatively charged ionomer is first deposited as a base coating to form a water-stable selective skin on a porous support membrane, followed by the application of a cationic polyelectrolyte to create a PEC TFC membrane. The proof-of-concept has been done with Nafion, a fluorinated sulfonic acid group containing ionomer that has originally been established as material for proton-exchange membranes [13]. By adjusting the polycation (e.g., polyvinylamine) concentration, resulting membrane charge and selectivity can be systematically controlled. This approach enables fine-tuning of separation performance characteristics without relying on labor-intensive LbL techniques. Nevertheless, concerns regarding the use of an expensive and unsus­tainable fluorinated ionomer and open questions related to precise control of molecular weight cut-off still remain.

The nanoscale morphology of ionomer films plays a pivotal role in determining their transport properties and separation performance. As reviewed by Li and Guiver, ion-conducting nanochannels formed by selfassembly in block or comb-shaped aromatic copolymers with ionexchange group containing segments provide efficient pathways for water and hydrated ions, enabling high ionic conductivity and selective transport, that is critical for electrochemical applications but can also be relevant for water purification [14]. These nano-scale morphologies typically result from phase separation between hydrophilic ionic do­mains and hydrophobic matrix blocks, that enables tuning of the ion transport mechanism, either via interstitial water pathways or hopping between charged groups, thereby impacting flux, selectivity, and chemical resistance. In a study with sulfonated aromatic multiblock copolymers and the focus on water desalination, Park et al. emphasized the importance of polymer backbone chemistry and block copolymer architecture for achieving competitive desalination performance and chlorine tolerance of ionomer TFC membranes [15]. Wieczorek and Ulbricht demonstrated that the positively charged couterparts, i.e.

Wieczorek and Ulbricht demonstrated that the positively charged couterparts, i.e. aro­matic multiblock copolymers with quaternary ammonium groups, can also be processed by film casting into nanochannel-in-matrix structures that yield TFC membranes with attractive intrinsic desalination perfor­mance and high chemical stability, including resistance to chlorine exposure [16]. In addition, Fischer et al. illustrated for cationic aromatic ionomers how the choice of casting solvent and post-treatment condi­tions (e.g., thermal or solvent annealing) can direct the formation of hydrophilic nanodomains in a hydrophobic matrix during membrane solidification, thereby offering another degree of control over mem­brane structure and function [17].

content: In this context, the sulfonated pentablock copolymer Nexar has garnered attention for its ability to form robust microphase-separated morphologies with high water uptake and tunable ion transport prop­erties [18]. It has also been demonstrated that Nexar membranes and ionomers are competitive with their fluorinated Nafion counterparts as proton-conducting materials for hydrogen fuel cell applications [13]. As shown by Geise et al., variations in sulfonation degree and casting conditions significantly influence water and salt permeability in Nexar films, highlighting the critical link between charge density, morphology, and separation behavior [19]. These features, combined with Nexar’s hydrocarbon-based backbone, render it a promising, scalable alternative to fluorinated ionomers like Nafion for the development of PEC-enabled NF membranes. Building on these advances, the aim of this study was to develop novel PEC-enabled nanofiltration membranes that are suitable for ter­tiary wastewater treatment, with one focus on achieving high rejections for organic micropollutants while ensuring stability of separation per­formance. The commercial ionomer Nexar was used in this work. In one previous study, Nexar had been coated onto a positively charged porous support membrane to obtain a NF membrane that had then been studied in terms of heavy metal ion rejection [20].

In this work, anionic Nexar was combined with branched polyethylenimine (PEI) as the polycation, and a scalable two-step dip-coating process was used to create PEC TFC membranes on an aminated polyacrylonitrile (aPAN) support mem­brane. Key variables such as solvent system, coating thickness, and post-treatments were examined to understand their impact on mem­brane morphology, structure, and separation performance. Detailed studies of the rejection of salt as well as sucrose and polyethyleneglycol (PEG) at varied conditions were performed to gain understanding regarding separation mechanisms. The rejection of various organic micropollutants was also studied. The membranes’ resistance to active chlorine exposure was investigated to demonstrate the oxidation sta­bility. Ultimately, this work contributes to bridging the gap between laboratory-scale PEC membrane innovations and scalable, robust membrane systems for real-world water reuse applications. 2. PAN support membrane fabrication The casting solution was prepared by gradually dissolving dry N-PAN into the appropriate solvent under continuous stirring. The recipe was 15 wt% N-PAN, 4 wt% LiCl, 7 wt% ethanol and 74 wt% DMSO, adapted from literature [21]. The mixture was then stored for at least 24 h at 80 ◦C in a convection oven, with intermittent manual stirring to ensure complete polymer dissolution. Membrane casting was carried out using a Philos pilot-scale casting machine (PHILOS, South Korea) at room temperature (20–22 ◦C) and moderate relative humidity (40–60 %).

content: carried out using a Philos pilot-scale casting machine (PHILOS, South Korea) at room temperature (20–22 ◦C) and moderate relative humidity (40–60 %). The nonwoven fabric served as the substrate, and the casting knife gap was set to 200 μm. The dope solution was applied at 7 cm/s, resulting in an air contact time of approximately 2.5 s before the film entered the coagulation bath containing water, positioned 20 cm downstream. After coagulation, the membrane was rinsed three times in DI water (con­taining 0.01 M NaN3) for 30 min and then stored overnight in the same solution to stabilize the structure. The amination reaction (see Fig. 2) was carried out as a one-pot process at 60 ◦C for 24 h in a 1 L 90:10 mixture of technical ethanol and DI water [22]. The reaction vessel was a 1 L Schott bottle. The membrane area was 600 cm2 (30 * 20 cm), and the membrane sheet was rolled with the non-woven side facing the glass wall of the bottle. Eth­ylenediamine (EDA) was added in a tenfold molar excess relative to the number of repeating units of PAN (deduced from PAN mass per mem­brane area). The reaction mixture was stirred at 500 rpm. After completion, the obtained aPAN membrane was washed with ethanol and stored in DI water (with 0.01 M NaN3). Before use as support for TFC membrane fabrication the membrane dried under ambient conditions after solvent exchange to ethanol.

For TFC membrane fabrication, dried Nexar was dissolved in the respective solvent (concentration provided in wt%) and stirred for at least 48 h prior to use. Three solvent systems were employed: a mixture of toluene and isopropanol (85:15 v/v) representing a non-polar solvent system with moderate vapor pressure, toluene and n-propanol (1:1 v/v) representing a somewhat more polar solvent system with a vapor pres­sure that is lower than that of first one, and tetrahydrofuran (THF), representing a moderately polar solvent with very high vapor pressure (see Supplementary Information, SI, section S1). The dip-coating process for preparing TFC membranes involved a controlled sequence of steps to apply a uniform polymer film onto the porous support membrane. First, the dry support membrane was mounted in the stainless steel frame and vertically immersed into the Nexar-containing polymer solution. After complete submersion, the membrane was kept in the solution for a defined impregnation time of 10 s to ensure sufficient wetting. Subsequently, the membrane was withdrawn from the solution at a controlled speed ranging from 2 to 15 mm/s, allowing a thin, uniform coating layer to form on the membrane surface.

For crosssectional analysis, the samples were cooled with liquid nitrogen and fractured. Measurements were performed in the range from pH ~3 to pH ~10, with each new pH value adjusted by adding a KOH so­lution. Rinsing time was 300 s for each step, ensuring accurate and stable pH values at the membrane surface. Streaming current was measured as a function of pressure difference (which is a function of flow rate). The data were automatically processed by the SurPASS software according to the Helmholtz-Smoluchowski equation to yield the zeta potential as function of pH value. Filtration setups, test conditions and determination of flux/ permeance Membrane performance was evaluated using two complementary nanofiltration (NF) systems: a small-scale dead-end NF system and a cross-flow NF unit. The dead-end filtration system was equipped with a magnetic stirrer to minimize concentration polarization. The dead-end cell had an effective membrane area of 9.62 cm2 and a total cell volume of 100 mL. Filtration experiments were conducted at a constant applied pressure of 15 bar. Prior to first pure water permeance (PWP) measurement, each membrane was pre-compacted for 30 min to ensure stabilization of flux. Prior to each subsequent filtration, membranes were thoroughly rinsed with the feed. Then 100 mL of feed were filled into the cell, filtration was started and the first ~2 mL of permeate was discarded to eliminate dead volume effects. Filtration proceeded thereafter until a total permeate volume of ~2 mL was collected. Complementary testing was performed using a cross-flow unit (CUBE 80-2 VA, SIMA-tec GmbH, Germany) with an effective membrane area of 84 cm2 and a feed volume of 2 L.

This setup enabled dynamic testing under continuous flow conditions, providing insights into solute rejec­tion and flux performance under more realistic operating scenarios including long-term evaluation. Applied pressure was 15 bar if not specified otherwise. The first measurement, conducted after a 30-min compaction phase, was determination of PWP, with data recorded over a 15-min period. Subsequently, the feed tank was emptied, a salt solution was introduced and the membrane was allowed to reach a steady state for at least 30 min until a stable conductivity value in the permeate was observed. A similar procedure was applied with organic solutes, with reaching steady state monitored using total organic carbon (TOC) analysis. Thereafter, the permeate flux was measured for an additional period of 10 min. Between each solute test, the membrane system was thoroughly rinsed with ultrapure water until the conduc­tivity or TOC values in the permeate approached baseline (pure water) levels. Subsequently, PWP was measured again for at least 10 min to assess membrane performance recovery and stability. Filtration tests were conducted using aqueous model solutions con­taining either inorganic salts (1 g/L) or organic solutes (0.2 g/L), depending on the specific test.

content: Pure water permeance (PWP) was calculated as follows: PWP =VA*t*ΔP With V as the volume of the permeate, A as the effective membrane area, t as the permeation time and ΔP as the applied transmembrane pressure. Salt solution permeance (SSP) was calculated considering the osmoctic pressure of the feed: SSP =VA*t*(ΔP -Π) With the osmotic pressure (Π): Π = i*c*R*T With i as the van ’t Hoff factor, c as the molar concentration of the solute, R as the universal gas constant and T as the temperature. The rejection of selected organic micropollutants (OMP) was inves­tigated using cross-flow filtration only. The following substances were selected as model OMP: sulfadiazine (1), sulfamethoxazole (2), sulfa­methazine (3), carbamazepine (4), ibuprofen (5), and triclosan (6). For the preparation of feed solutions, 50 mg of each OMP was first dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol and subsequently diluted into 5 L of ultrapure water. Two sets of mixed solutions were prepared: one containing OMP 1–4 (“Mix 1”), and another containing OMP 5&6 (“Mix 2”). In addition to testing a mixture of OMP, individual experiments were performed for each compound as well. For these single-solute tests, time-resolved measurements were carried out to monitor rejection behavior and assess potential adsorption onto the membrane or system components.

Reproducibility and error of flux and rejection data For all membrane types, at least two coupons obtained from one membrane sheet were tested. In the cases where only such two mea­surements had been performed, the data for permeance, MWCO or so­lute rejection are reported as average value without error range/bars. The difference between the two values was less than 5 %. In all other cases, at least 3 coupons from one membrane sheet or at least 4 coupons from two membrane sheets were tested. The respective data for per­meance, MWCO or solute rejection are reported as average value along with error calculated as the standard deviation from the average value. 3. Results and discussion This section is organized thematically to follow the membrane development process. It first covers fabrication and post-modification of the PAN support membranes. It then details formation and optimization of the Nexar barrier film to yield TFC membranes. Influence of dip-coating conditions on Nexar TFC membranes To optimize the formation of the selective Nexar film in TFC mem­branes, the effects of dip-coating parameters, including the solvent system, withdrawal speed and evaporation rate were systematically investigated. These parameters were varied to study how they influence membrane morphology and, consequently, separation performance. The aim was to identify processing conditions that promote the formation of a uniform, defect-free selective film enabling high water permeance and effective solute rejection. 3.2.1. Effects of solvent choice and evaporation rate To evaluate how solvents affect the formation and performance of the Nexar film, membranes were prepared using three solvent systems: toluene:isopropanol (85:15 v%), toluene:n-propanol (50:50 v%), and pure tetrahydrofuran (THF), all with 3 wt% polymer concentration and processed under the same dip-coating and slow drying conditions. These solvents differ in polarity and Hansen solubility parameters, as well as volatility (see section S1 in SI), which influences how the block copol­ymer segments self-assemble during film formation. Nexar, being an amphiphilic block copolymer with sulfonated hydrophilic blocks and hydrophobic blocks, can microphase-separate into different morphol­ogies depending on solvent quality for each block [18,26–30]. The results show substantial differences in separation performance (Table 2).

content: nt morphol­ogies depending on solvent quality for each block [18,26–30]. The results show substantial differences in separation performance (Table 2). The toluene:isopropanol system yielded a balanced mem­brane with moderate water permeance (2.2 LMH/bar), good sucrose rejection (~55 %) and the highest NaCl rejection (78 %). The membrane cast from n-propanol:toluene showed a much higher PWP (~3.9 LMH/ bar) but very poor rejection for sucrose (~20 %) and NaCl (~5 %). The THF-cast membrane had low PWP (~1.1 LMH/bar) and good sucrose rejection (~65 %), but low rejection for NaCl (35 %). These observations indicate that solvent polarity and evaporation behavior strongly influ­ence the final membrane structure. The toluene:n-propanol system, that is not very volatile, likely created a well solvating environment that favored the formation and expansion of ionic domains in a slowly so­lidifying hydrophobic matrix, leading to hydrophilic pathways that allow sucrose (~342 Da; size ≤1 nm) to pass almost without hindrance and that do not hinder passage of hydrated Na+ and Cl+ ions. In contrast, THF, a fast-evaporating, good solvent for both blocks, may have pro­moted phase separation to yield size-selective pores (higher rejection for sucrose than for NaCl), but porosity/pore-connectivity or accumulation of charged groups in the pores were likely not well-developed.

The balanced separation performance of the membrane from the toluene: isopropanol system suggests that this allows the best self-assembly to­ward a channel-in-matrix morphology with size-sieving properties and charged groups in the pores (higher rejection for NaCl than for sucrose). To evaluate the impact of evaporation rate on membrane structure and performance, Nexar films were dip-coated from a toluene:iso­propanol (85:15 v%) solution and then subjected to two drying condi­tions: covered with aluminum foil (“slow”; duration ~12 h) and uncovered (“fast”; duration ~2 h). All other parameters, including polymer concentration and withdrawal speed, were kept constant. Remarkably, all performance indicators deteriorated largely after fast drying, PWP declined from 2.2 to 1.2 LMH/bar, and the rejection values for sucrose and NaCl declined from 55 to 30 %, and from 78 to 65 %, respectively. This performance difference is attributed to a significantly different film formation process. Slow evaporation provides sufficient time for the sulfonated segments of the Nexar copolymer to self-organize into a percolating network of hydrophilic domains embedded in a cocontinuous hydrophobic matrix.

This channel-in-matrix structure facil­itates water transport while maintaining size exclusion for solutes such as sucrose (~342 Da) and enabling a significant additional effect of charged groups in the pores on salt rejection (this will be investigated in more detail in subsequent sections). In contrast, rapid solvent evapo­ration likely leads to kinetic trapping of polymer in a less ordered morphology, limiting domain connectivity and potentially creating nonselective pathways. As a result, the fast-dried membrane has lower so­lute rejection and lower permeance. These findings are consistent with results of prior studies on ionomer thin films, where slower solvent evaporation has been shown to reduce defect density and enhance ionic domain connectivity [31,32]. Exactly the same trends, major increases of PWP and solute rejection, have also been observed for TFC mem­branes with positively charged ionomer films after the cast films were dried at lower temperature, i.e., slower rate [16]. Notably, for such self-assembling ionomer systems, the improved permeance does not necessarily come at the cost of solute rejection; slow solidification allows to achieve a much more favorable balance of permeability and selectivity.

Effect of dip coating speed Besides solvent effects, the thickness of the Nexar coating (controlled by the viscosity and surface tension of the solution and the dip-coating withdrawal speed) is another important parameter. In dip-coating, higher withdrawal speeds generally entrain a thicker liquid film, lead­ing to a thicker dried film, whereas lower speeds yield thinner films. However, very low speeds can lead to non-uniform films due to gravi­tational drainage and instabilities [33]. A series of membranes was prepared using the toluene:isopropanol solvent with varying withdrawal speeds (i.e., 120, 520, and 820 mm/min). The measured dry film thickness (obtained from cross-sectional SEM images of the TFC mem­branes; see section S2 in SI) and resulting PWP and solute rejections are given in Table 3. After using 120 mm/min, the Nexar film was ~0.40 μm thick; for 820 mm/min, thickness increased to ~1.5 μm (nearly 3 *thicker). Intuitively, one would expect the thinnest film to yield the TFC membrane with highest water permeance (since it presents the lowest transport resistance), but possibly the lowest rejection if defects are present. Surprisingly, the obtained results did not show a simple inverse relationship between film thickness and flux. The thickest film (~1.5 μm, for 820 mm/min) actually yielded the highest permeance (2.2 LMH/bar) followed by the medium thickness (~0.8 μm, for 520 mm/min: 1.3 LMH/bar) and the thinnest film (0.4 μm, for 120 mm/min: 0.5 LMH/bar).

content: nce (2.2 LMH/bar) followed by the medium thickness (~0.8 μm, for 520 mm/min: 1.3 LMH/bar) and the thinnest film (0.4 μm, for 120 mm/min: 0.5 LMH/bar). Furthermore, the thickest film also achieved the highest sucrose rejection (~54 %), whereas the thinnest film yielded only ~22 % sucrose rejection. The NaCl rejection trend (820 mm/min: ~79 %, 520 mm/min: ~57 %, 120 mm/min: ~65 %) is somewhat non-monotonic, but generally the highest salt rejection was also observed for the thickest film. These trends suggest that film quality and microstructure are more important than minimizing thickness. It ap­pears that the 120 mm/min coating (very thin) may have suffered from support membrane pore blockage as well as incomplete support surface coverage or microscopic defects, overall drastically reducing its per­formance. The moderate speed (520 mm/min, ~0.8 μm) produced a thicker but perhaps still imperfect film. The highest speed (820 mm/min) yielded a robust, thick film that paradoxically yielded the best of both: highest flux and highest solute rejection. It appears that the high speed helped achieve a superior film because viscous drag dominates and facilitates the creation of a smooth film. At a thickness of ~1.5 μm, the Nexar film is likely to form a continuous, extrinsic defect-free network of ionic domains, which facilitates water transport via con­nected hydrophilic channels while still maintaining a relatively tight pore size for solute rejection [20].

Thicker films might also tolerate minor intrinsic defects without compromising overall rejection, as de­fects would be buried under more material. This finding highlights that simply minimizing film thickness does not guarantee better separation performance; instead ensuring the structural integrity of the selective film is critical. Similar observations were made by Zelner et al., who noted that an optimal polyelectrolyte complex film thickness, obtained by coating the ionomer Nafion in the first step, existed where flux was maximized without sacrificing rejection [12]. 3.2.3. After exposure of Nexar TFC membranes to THF vapor notable changes were indeed observed. The pure water permeance roughly doubled (from ~2.2 to ~4 LMH/bar). It is inferred that the Nexar morphology may have transitioned towards a more ordered but also more porous structure, likely a lamellar structure with larger continuous channel domains. Prior work supports this interpretation: Mineart et al. showed that solvent annealing a sulfonated block copolymer could switch its morphology from cylindrical to lamellar, increasing water uptake [30]. In case of this study, such a lamellar structure might form larger straight channels through the film, boosting flux but also allowing more sucrose passage (hence lower rejection).

This flux-selectivity trade-off highlights the challenge in membrane optimization: modifications that enhance flux (via increasing pore size or connectivity) often also increase the likelihood of solute passage. Thermal annealing at 60 ◦C in water for 7 days resulted in a signif­icant improvement in solute rejection across all tested substances. For instance, sucrose rejection increased from approximately 50 % to 60 %, while Na2SO4 rejection surpassed 90 %. This slight enhancement is attributed to the formation of a denser membrane structure, likely caused by polymer domain reorganization during the hot water treat­ment. The improved rejection, particularly for neutral organic solutes and multivalent salts, suggests a reduced MWCO. However, this came at the cost of reduced membrane permeance, which decreased to 1.5 LMH/ bar (see section S4 in SI), due to the tighter morphology. This enhancement is phenomeno­logically attributed to the introduction of positively charged PEI, which moderates the influence of the negatively charged Nexar. By depositing PEI onto the sulfonated Nexar surface, a mixed-charge structure is created, potentially shifting the membrane surface toward neutrality or even a net-positive charge. This mixed-charge character favors the exclusion of cations, particularly multivalent ones, via Donnan exclu­sion. Importantly, what is referred to here as „charge density“ includes contributions from both fixed ionic groups (sulfonic acid in Nexar, protonated amine in PEI) and mobile counter-ions present within the hydrated channels in the membrane matrix. Zeta potential measure­ments (discussed in Section 3.5) support this interpretation, showing that PEI-25k in particular reduces the negative surface charge relative to the unmodified TFC. Consequently, divalent cations such as Ca2+ and Mg2+ experience weaker electrostatic attaction in the PEC-coated membranes, enhancing their rejection [34]. Notably, NaCl rejection did not improve as much with PEI coating, in fact at 1 g/L NaCl, TFC had ~70 % rejection, while PEC membranes had around 70–80 %.

content: ably, NaCl rejection did not improve as much with PEI coating, in fact at 1 g/L NaCl, TFC had ~70 % rejection, while PEC membranes had around 70–80 %. In addition to Donnan effects, the improved rejection of multivalent salts can be further explained by combined contributions of steric and dielectric exclusion mechanisms [6]. Steric hindrance arises from the reduced effective pore size caused by PEI deposition, as revealed by higher sucrose rejection. Dielectric exclusion, which becomes significant when the membrane pores are small and the polymer matrix has a low dielectric constant compared to water, results from the energetic penalty associated with partial ion dehydration upon entry into a low-permittivity environment. Unlike Donnan exclusion, dielectric exclusion affects both cations and anions equally, more strongly the multivalent ions, and is independent of the membrane’s net charge. The major increase in rejection of multivalent salts, along with almost un­changed NaCl rejection, is characteristic of dielectric exclusion. A notable effect of the PEI coating was also observed regarding pure water permeance (PWP). Compared to the baseline TFC membrane (2.0 LMH/bar), the PWP decreased to 1.25 LMH/bar with the PEI-600 coating, consistent with the formation of a denser selective layer due to internal charge compensation, but also a consequence of increased film thickness (cf. Section 3.5). Interestingly, the PEI-25k membrane exhibited an increased PWP of 2.7 LMH/bar.

This suggests that the high molecular weight PEI subtly alters the film structure in a way that fa­cilitates water transport (cf. Section 3.5). These distinct behaviors un­derscore the importance of polyelectrolyte molecular weight and interaction mode in tailoring membrane performance. Overall, at 1 g/L solute concentration, the salt rejection observed for the PEC NF mem­branes was higher than that for the TFC membranes for all salts, demonstrating the value of the PEC approach in increasing solute rejection and modulating ion-selectivity. Crucially, the long-term chemical stability of these membranes under active chlorine cleaning/disinfection conditions was also assessed (Fig. 6). Nexar TFC and PEC membranes were subjected to continuous filtration with a 500 ppm NaOCl solution (containing 2 g/L NaCl, pH 9) for up to 2 days. No significant decline in membrane performance, regarding flux or salt rejection, was observed for any of the membranes. The Nexar TFC membranes retained salt rejection and PWP within experimental error. The PEC membranes interestingly revealed in in­crease in permeance by about 20 % while rejection remained un­changed. Overall, this indicates a high resistance to hypochlorite attack.

This stability is attributed to the chemical composition of the selective films: Nexar is a hydrocarbon polymer containing sulfonic acid groups, and PEI is a polyamine; neither polymer contains the aromatic amide bonds that are the Achilles’ heel of polyamide membranes. For example, van Do et al. reported that even 1 ppm of chlorine could cut the life of a polyamide NF membrane significantly [7]. Prior studies have also shown that sulfonated poly(arylene) membranes and polyelectrolyte multilayer membranes can withstand active chlorine much better than polyamides [15,16,35]. The findings of this study emphasize that using an ionomer-based PEC membrane is a viable strategy for applications requiring active chlorine usage.

These complementary techniques were selected to elucidate the effects of PEC formation onto membrane structure, and to distinguish between the film-embedded and surface-localized PEI configurations evoked above. The Nexar TFC membrane shows a negative zeta potential across the pH range 3–10. At low pH (~3), the zeta potential is slightly less negative (around -30 mV) because some sulfonic acid groups may be protonated. As pH increases, the sulfonic acid groups deprotonate fully and impart a strong negative charge (zeta potential reaching about -100 mV at pH 10).

This confirms that the Nexar film in the TFC membranes is anionic (negatively charged). To ensure that zeta potential data are reproducible, second batches of all membranes had been analyzed (see Fig. S7, Section S3 in SI), and the results confirm that the differences between the three membranes are significant. The PEC PEI-600 membrane, interestingly, does not show a positive zeta potential despite the presence of PEI, a weak polycation, with amino groups that are protonated (positively charged) in the lower pH range. Its zeta potential curve is even slightly more in the negative range than that of the TFC membrane. This difference is considered significant because the same was observed for a second batch of TFC and subse­quently prepared PEC membrane (cf. Fig. S7). While this appears counterintuitive, one plausible explanation is that the low-molecularweight PEI diffuses into the Nexar matrix and binds to internal sul­fonic acid groups, forming a homogeneous PEC throughout the film.

Influence of feed and filtration conditions on separation performance To gain deeper insight into the solute rejection behavior, experi­ments were conducted covering a range of feed concentrations (from 0.2 up to 10 g/L) for various salts using the three membrane types. Fig. 9shows the rejection as a function of salt concentration for NaCl, Na2SO4, CaCl2, and MgSO4 for Nexar TFC vs PEC PEI-600 and PEC PEI-25k membranes. At low salt concentration (~0.2–1 g/L), all membranes exhibit the highest rejections. The net negatively charged NF mem­branes (cf. Fig. 7) operate predominantly under Donnan exclusion in this regime: the fixed charges in the membrane repel co-ions (anions) and attract counter-ions, leading to high rejection of salts, especially those with multivalent co-ions [37]. As the feed salt concentration increases, a decline in rejection is observed for all salts and all membranes. For example, NaCl rejection for TFC drops from ~85 % at 0.2 g/L to ~50 % at 5 g/L and ~20 % at 10 g/L. The PEC membranes similarly show decreasing rejection, though they maintain higher absolute rejection than TFC at each concentration.

content: ~20 % at 10 g/L. The PEC membranes similarly show decreasing rejection, though they maintain higher absolute rejection than TFC at each concentration. Importantly, the changes are completely reversible, as confirmed by idential NaCl rejection at 1 g/L feed concentration after filtrations had been performed at concentra­tions up to 10 g/L (see section S7 in SI). While this behavior is often interpreted in terms of electrostatic screening of fixed charges and thereby reducing the magnitude of the Donnan effect, this explanation is not entirely applicable to ionomeror PEC-based membranes. Instead, the dominant mechanism appears to be increased salt partitioning into the membrane, as intrinsic charge compensation, i.e., pairing of fixed charged groups of Nexar and PEI, is gradually overtaken by extrinsic compensation by mobile ions of the salt from the external solution, an effect commonly referred to as the „anti-polyelectrolyte“ effect. This phenomenon leads to greater salt uptake and internal swelling, which in turn reduces salt rejection and enhances permeance [12]. Indeed, the rejections of TFC, PEI-600, and PEI-25k membranes start to converge at the highest concentrations, indicating that the advantage of forming the PEC with PEI diminishes when charge screening is strong. Nonetheless, even at 10 g/L, the PEC membranes still show modestly higher rejection than TFC ones (for MgSO4, ~40 % vs 30 %; for NaCl, ~35 % vs 20 %).

content: Nonetheless, even at 10 g/L, the PEC membranes still show modestly higher rejection than TFC ones (for MgSO4, ~40 % vs 30 %; for NaCl, ~35 % vs 20 %). This suggests the PEC membranes have a more robust exclusion capacity across a range of ionic conditions, likely due to their combination of charge and smaller pore size. Looking at differences among the salts: Na2SO4 consistently has the highest rejection on all membranes at all concentrations, owing to the divalent sulfate anion being strongly excluded by Donnan effects and also contributions of size exclusion and dielectric exclusion (sulfate has a larger hydrated radius than Cl-). CaCl2 is less rejected because the cation is multivalent. MgSO4 and NaCl show intermediate rejections, with NaCl usually above MgSO4. These detailed ion-specific effects are complex, but generally follow known NF trends [38,39]. To summarize, the salt rejection results indicate that the membranes operate under a Donnan exclusion domi­nated regime at low concentration. At higher salt concentration, rejec­tion is increasingly governed by salt uptake and swelling within the membrane. The conversion of the Nexar to a PEI-PEC film shifts the performance to higher rejection for all solutes, acting like a membrane with a more neutral charge pattern and smaller pores, that also increase the effect of dielectric exclusion. The observations in this study agree with the trends reported by Zelner et al.

While the Donnan model is useful for interpreting ion rejection through electrostatic effects, it does not explain the observed increase in solution permeance with increasing salt concentrations. Nexar contains sulfonic acid group-containing segments (cf. Fig. 1) that cluster in the hydrophilic domains that function as selective pathways through the membrane (cf. Section 3.2). The increased salt partitioning and connected water uptake leads to swelling, resulting in increased free space within the membrane’s charged domains, reducing resistance to pressure-driven water transport. Indeed, the extent of permeance in­crease varied with salt type, reflecting differences in ion valence and hydration, and resulting interactions with the membrane. NaCl exhibi­ted the most pronounced permeance increase. In contrast, multivalent salts such as Na2SO4, CaCl2, and MgSO4 caused only moderate increases in permeance. This cannot directly be related to the extent of their partitioning into the membrane because, e.g., at the same high molar salt concentration the passage of NaCl was lower than that of CaCl2, i.e., the salt concentration in the membrane was lower for NaCl. Membrane structure also significantly influenced how permeance responded to salt concentration. The TFC membrane displayed the steepest increase in permeance for NaCl, consistent with its relatively open pore structure and high sensitivity to ionic screening of the charges of sulfonic acid groups in the channels. The PEI-600 membrane showed a more uniform and lower response across all salts.

In order to further clarify the influence of the barrier film on solute rejection, nanofiltration experiments were conducted using sucrose so­lutions at varying NaCl concentrations (see Fig. 11). Importantly, su­crose rejection remained constant across all NaCl concentrations, maintaining the same value observed for sucrose-only tests for each membrane type (TFC, PEI-600, and PEI-25k). In contrast, NaCl rejection decreased and solution permeance increased with increasing NaCl concentration (see section S8 in SI), consistent with the single-solute experiments (cf. Figs. 9&10).
